---
title: "Demo Reels"
guide-section: "Site Content"
bread-crumbs: false
tags: [Content, Video, Multimedia, AI]
versions: ">=0.14"
upcoming: "0.15.0"
status: experimental
---

# Demo Reels

Great Docs includes **Showreel**, a way to script narrated demo videos and embed them straight into
your documentation. You write a small YAML file describing a sequence of scenes, and Showreel
synthesizes the voiceover, composes a timeline, and renders it as a crisp in-page player. The same
reel also exports to MP4, WebM, or an animated GIF.

Where [Terminal Recordings](../user-guide/terminal-recordings.qmd) document the terminal, Showreel
documents everything else: title cards, code walkthroughs, live web apps, and executed notebooks,
all stitched together with a generated narration track and word-synced captions. Nothing leaves your
machine, and the web player works offline with no external hosting.

Here is a reel embedded in a page. It opens with a title card, walks through a code example with a
"magic-move" transition, shows the same table before and after `data_color` so the color washes into
the column, and closes on a call to action:

{{< showreel file="assets/showreel-demo" >}}

Five scenes make up this opener: a title, a magic-move code block, the plain and colored table
images, and an outro card. It builds offline with the silent voice. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
# A small narrated demo reel used by the Demo Reels user-guide page.
# Builds fully offline (silent voice, no capture) so it renders anywhere.
# Set voice.engine to piper for a real voiceover locally.
showreel:
  title: "data_color()"
  description: "A short tour of Great Tables' data_color() method."
  theme: auto
  brand:
    accent: "#2563eb"

voice:
  engine: silent

# The opener showcases the full transport (scrubber, ticks, speed, captions).
player:
  controls: full

defaults:
  transition: crossfade
  captions: true
  lead_in: 0.5
  motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.06, from: center, to: top-left }

scenes:
  - id: intro
    type: title
    title: "data_color()"
    subtitle: "Color scales for any column"
    say: "Great Tables turns a column of numbers into a color gradient."
    motion: { type: none }

  - id: code
    type: code
    language: python
    say: "One method call does it, and you can pick any palette."
    steps:
      - code: |
          from great_tables import GT
          GT(data)
        typing: true
      - code: |
          from great_tables import GT

          (
            GT(data)
            .data_color(
              columns="population",
              palette="viridis"
            )
          )
        focus: [5, 6, 7, 8]

  # Before/after: the same table portion, plain then color-scaled. Both images
  # are identical in size and framing (fit: contain, motion: none), so the
  # crossfade reads as the color washing into the Population column.
  - id: table-plain
    type: image
    src: reel-datacolor-before.png
    fit: contain
    motion: { type: none }
    say: "Here's a column of plain numbers."

  - id: table-color
    type: image
    src: reel-datacolor-after.png
    fit: contain
    motion: { type: none }
    say: "data_color turns it into a gradient you can read at a glance."

  - id: outro
    type: card
    title: "Try it"
    body: "pip install great-tables"
    cta: "posit-dev.github.io/great-tables"
    say: "Install it today and start coloring."
```
:::

The player above renders from a single spec file. Press the play button (or {{< keys "Space" >}}) to
watch, use the scrubber to jump around, and toggle captions with the CC button. Everything you see,
including the caption timing and the code transition, is driven by one deterministic clock, so the
exported video looks identical to this player.

## Quick Start

The fastest way to understand Showreel is to make a reel. These four steps take you from an empty
file to an embedded, narrated player. The rest of this page then fills in each piece in depth.

### 1. Scaffold a reel

Create a starter spec next to your other assets. Reels live in `.showreel.yml` files anywhere in
your project:

```bash
great-docs showreel new reels/getting-started
```

This writes `reels/getting-started.showreel.yml` with a title, an image scene, and an outro card to
edit.

### 2. Write the scenes

Open the file and describe your scenes. Each scene pairs one visual with one line of narration. The
narration length sets the scene duration when you leave `duration` at its default:

```{.yaml filename="reels/getting-started.showreel.yml"}
showreel:
  title: "Getting started"
  theme: auto

voice:
  engine: piper            # local voice; falls back to silent if not installed

scenes:
  - id: intro
    type: title
    title: "My Package"
    subtitle: "Do more with less code"
    say: "Here is a two minute tour of what you can build."
    motion: { type: none }

  - id: demo
    type: code
    language: python
    say: "It takes a single import and one function call."
    steps:
      - code: "import mypkg"
        typing: true
      - code: |
          import mypkg
          mypkg.run(config="fast")
        focus: [2]
```

### 3. Preview it

Build the reel and open the player in your browser:

```bash
great-docs showreel preview reels/getting-started
```

The command builds the reel, starts a small local server, and opens the player. Edit the YAML,
rebuild, and refresh to iterate.

### 4. Embed or export

Reference the reel from any `.qmd` page with the shortcode, using the path without the extension:

```markdown
{{</* showreel file="reels/getting-started" */>}}
```

To produce a downloadable video instead, export it:

```bash
great-docs showreel export reels/getting-started --format mp4
```

With the basics in place, the rest of this page covers each scene type, the voice and caption
options, motion and overlays, embedding, exporting, and the Studio editor.

## Scenes

Scenes are the building blocks of a reel. Each one holds a single visual and a single line of
narration, and the build lays them end to end into one timeline. You mix and match the types freely,
so a single reel can open on a title card, walk through some code, show a screenshot, and close on a
call to action. The reel below moves through several types in a row.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-scenes" >}}

Four scene types in sequence, a title, an image, a code block, and a card, narrated with Piper. Here
is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Scene types"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb", logo: logo.svg }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults:
  lead_in: 0.5
  transition: crossfade
  captions: true
  motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.06 }
scenes:
  - id: intro
    type: title
    title: "Scene types"
    subtitle: "One reel, many kinds of scene"
    say: "A reel is a sequence of scenes. Here are a few of the kinds."
    motion: { type: none }
  - id: img
    type: image
    src: city-florence.jpg
    say: "An image scene, with a gentle pan and zoom."
  - id: code
    type: code
    language: python
    say: "A code scene, highlighted at build time."
    steps:
      - code: |
          from great_tables import GT
          GT(data)
  - id: card
    type: card
    title: "And more"
    body: "web pages, notebooks, and cards"
    say: "Plus live web pages, notebooks, and title cards."
```
:::

A reel is an ordered list of scenes under the `scenes:` key. Every scene has an `id`, a `type`, and
usually a `say` line for narration. Showreel supports six scene types:

| Type       | Visual                                   | Notes                                       |
| ---------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `title`    | A large title and subtitle               | Good for openers and section dividers       |
| `card`     | A title, body line, and call to action   | Good for outros and takeaways               |
| `image`    | A full-bleed image                       | A pan and zoom brings it to life          |
| `figure`   | A visual with a chunk of text below it   | Pairs a result with a short explanation   |
| `code`     | Syntax-highlighted code                  | Magic-move between steps, optional typing   |
| `web`      | A live web page captured in Chrome       | Runs an interaction script, then captures   |
| `notebook` | An executed marimo notebook              | Runs the notebook and captures the output   |

Every reel is some combination of these six types. The rest of this section takes each one in turn,
starting with the two that need no assets at all.

### Title and card scenes

Title and card scenes are the punctuation of a reel: the opener that names the topic, the dividers
between chapters, and the outro that tells the viewer what to do next. They render entirely from
text, so they need no assets and build instantly. A title scene takes `title` and `subtitle`; a card
scene adds `body` and `cta` fields:

```{.yaml}
- id: outro
  type: card
  title: "Get started"
  body: "pip install my-package"
  cta: "my-docs-site.example"
  say: "Install it today."
```

Because they are pure text, these scenes cost nothing to render and stay crisp at any resolution.
They are the safest place to start when you are blocking out a reel and still deciding what the
content scenes will hold.

### Image scenes

An image scene is the simplest way to put something real on screen: a screenshot, a diagram, a
photograph, or a rendered result. Great Docs copies the file into the reel bundle at build time, so
the finished player stays self-contained.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-image" >}}

A single image scene with a slow pan and zoom. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Image scene"
  theme: auto
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
# The play button sits on the presentation; click the image to play/pause.
player: { play_button: overlay }
defaults: { lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: img
    type: image
    src: city-toronto-nighttime.png
    say: "An image fills the frame, with a slow pan and zoom to bring it to life."
    motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.12, from: bottom-right, to: top-left }
```
:::

An image scene shows a picture and, by default, applies a gentle pan and zoom. Point `src`
at a file relative to the spec; Showreel copies it into the built bundle:

```{.yaml}
- id: screenshot
  type: image
  src: assets/dashboard.png
  say: "This is the dashboard you will build."
  motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.08, from: center, to: top-right }
```

By default an image fills the frame (`fit: cover`), which suits full-bleed photos. For a table,
chart, or diagram that must be seen whole, set `fit: contain` so the image is shown complete on the
stage background rather than cropped. This is how the opening reel shows its table: two image scenes
of the same table portion, one plain and one with `data_color` applied, each with `fit: contain` and
`motion: { type: none }`. Because the two images share the same size and framing, the crossfade
between them reads as the color washing into the column:

```{.yaml}
- id: table-plain
  type: image
  src: assets/table-before.png
  fit: contain
  motion: { type: none }
  say: "Here's a column of plain numbers."
- id: table-color
  type: image
  src: assets/table-after.png
  fit: contain
  motion: { type: none }
  say: "data_color turns it into a gradient you can read at a glance."
```

:::{.callout-tip}
To capture a Great Tables table as an image for a reel, use its `gtsave()` method:
`GT(df).data_color(columns="population", palette="viridis").gtsave("assets/table-after.png")`. Render
the plain and colored versions the same way so the frames line up.
:::

Reach for an image scene whenever the thing you want to show is already a picture, or can be saved as
one. They build instantly, need no browser, and pair well with the motion effects covered later on
this page.

### Figure scenes

Documentation often pairs a result with a sentence about it. A figure scene does exactly that: it
stacks a visual over a chunk of prose, so the reader sees the output and reads the point at the same
time. It is the on-screen counterpart to narration, and it needs no voice.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-figure" >}}

Two figures, each a Great Tables result with a short explanation beneath it, in the style of the
package's own README. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
# Demonstrates the `figure` scene: a visual stacked over a chunk of prose, the
# way the Great Tables README pairs a result with a short explanation. No voice;
# the reader reads the text on screen.
showreel:
  title: "Figure scenes"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }

voice:
  engine: silent

defaults:
  transition: crossfade

scenes:
  - id: labels
    type: figure
    src: reel-figure-table.png
    text: "It's okay to have repeating column labels. Functions like `cols_label()` and `cols_label_with()` make it so easy too."

  - id: color
    type: figure
    src: reel-figure-table2.png
    text: "Add a color scale to any column with a single `data_color()` call, using any palette you like."
```
:::

Point `src` at the visual and put the explanation in `text`. The text accepts inline Markdown, so
`` `code` `` renders in your brand accent, and `**bold**` and `*italic*` work as you would expect:

```{.yaml}
- id: labels
  type: figure
  src: assets/table.png
  text: "It's okay to have repeating column labels. `cols_label()` makes it easy."
```

With no `say` line the scene is silent and paced to give the reader time to read; add a `say` line to
narrate it as well. The visual is shown whole by default (`fit: contain`); set `fit: cover` to fill
the frame with a photo. Figure scenes are ideal for turning a static README or slide deck into a reel
without recording a voiceover.

### Code scenes

Code is the heart of most library demos, and a code scene makes it move. Instead of a static block,
you show the reader how a snippet grows, one line at a time, with the change spotlighted as it lands.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-code" >}}

This reel magic-moves through a Great Tables snippet, spotlighting the three `tab_spanner` calls as
they are added, then crossfades to the table it renders. It has no voiceover: the code and its output
carry the scene. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
# Shown in the Code scenes section of the Demo Reels guide. A magic-move through
# a Great Tables snippet, then a crossfade to the table it renders. No voice: the
# code and its output carry the scene.
showreel:
  title: "Code to output"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }

voice:
  engine: silent

defaults:
  transition: crossfade
  captions: false

scenes:
  - id: code
    type: code
    language: python
    duration: 8.0
    steps:
      - code: |
          from great_tables import GT, md
          from great_tables.data import towny

          cols = ["name", "latitude", "longitude", "population_2016",
                  "density_2016", "population_2021", "density_2021"]
          top5 = towny.nlargest(5, "population_2021")[cols]

          GT(top5)
        typing: true
        note: "Start with the five most populous cities."
      - code: |
          from great_tables import GT, md
          from great_tables.data import towny

          cols = ["name", "latitude", "longitude", "population_2016",
                  "density_2016", "population_2021", "density_2021"]
          top5 = towny.nlargest(5, "population_2021")[cols]

          (
              GT(top5)
              .tab_spanner("Population", ["population_2016", "population_2021"])
              .tab_spanner("Density", ["density_2016", "density_2021"])
              .tab_spanner(md("*Location*"), ["latitude", "longitude"], id="loc")
          )
        focus: [10, 11, 12]
        note: "Three `tab_spanner()` calls group the columns under shared headers."

  - id: output
    type: image
    src: reel-code-output.png
    fit: contain
    duration: 6.5
    motion: { type: none }
    # Region callouts point at parts of the rendered table; the note dodges into
    # the clear space beside each region. Rects are relative to the image.
    annotate:
      - rect: [0.44, 0.04, 0.26, 0.20]
        note: "`tab_spanner()` groups two columns under one header."
        at: 0.6
        duration: 2.6
      - rect: [0.20, 0.04, 0.24, 0.20]
        note: "`md(\"*Location*\")` even italicizes the label."
        at: 3.4
        duration: 2.6
```
:::

Code scenes are highlighted at build time with Pygments, so the player stays lightweight and works
offline. Provide a single `code` block, or a list of `steps` that the player "magic-moves" between.
Set `typing: true` to reveal a step as if it were being typed, and `focus` to spotlight the changed
lines:

```{.yaml}
- id: build-up
  type: code
  language: python
  say: "Start simple, then add the color scale."
  steps:
    - code: "GT(data)"
      typing: true
    - code: |
        (GT(data)
           .data_color(columns="pop", palette="viridis"))
      focus: [2]
```

Keep each step to a small, runnable change so the magic-move stays legible; a step that rewrites half
the block reads as a jump cut rather than a build. The narration paces the steps, so you rarely need
to set a duration by hand.

Give a step an on-screen `note` to explain it without a voiceover. The note appears in a soft,
blurred box that automatically dodges the code: it docks on the side away from the focused lines, and
the code shrinks and shifts to make room. The text accepts inline Markdown, so `` `code` `` picks up
your brand accent:

```{.yaml}
steps:
  - code: "GT(data)"
    note: "Start with the raw table."
  - code: |
      (GT(data)
         .data_color(columns="pop", palette="viridis"))
    focus: [2]
    note: "One `data_color()` call adds the color scale."
```

Placement is automatic; set `note_side: top` or `note_side: bottom` to override it. Notes are the
on-screen alternative to narration, so a scene that uses them usually sets `captions: false` (or omits
`say`). The reel above uses a note on each step.

### Web scenes

A web scene records a real page doing real things. It drives an actual browser through a script you
write, captures a frame at each step, and plays those frames back in sequence, so the reader watches
your interface respond rather than a mockup.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-web" >}}

One web scene, captured live in Chrome as a synthetic cursor drives it through the page. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Web capture"
  theme: dark
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: app
    type: web
    url: reel-explorer.html
    viewport: { width: 1280, height: 760, scale: 1 }
    cursor: synthetic
    say: "A live web page, captured in a real browser as the cursor drives it through each step."
    steps:
      - wait_for: "#go"
      - capture: { label: "start" }
      - click: "#go"
      - capture: { label: "recolored" }
      - select: { selector: "#palette", value: "magma" }
      - capture: { label: "magma" }
```
:::

A web scene drives a real page in headless Chrome and captures keyframes as it goes. List the
interaction as `steps`; each `capture` step records a frame. Set `cursor: synthetic` to overlay an
animated pointer that moves to the elements you click:

```{.yaml}
- id: live-app
  type: web
  url: "http://localhost:8000/explorer"
  viewport: { width: 1440, height: 900, scale: 2 }
  cursor: synthetic
  say: "Swap the palette and the whole table recolors."
  steps:
    - wait_for: "#table"
    - capture: { label: "The explorer" }
    - select: { selector: "#palette", value: "magma" }
    - wait_for: ".recolored"
    - capture: { label: "Magma palette" }
```

The available step actions are `wait_for` (a CSS selector), `wait` (seconds), `click`, `fill`
(`{selector, text}`), `select` (`{selector, value}`), `scroll`, `eval` (a JavaScript expression),
and `capture` (`{label}`).

:::{.callout-note}
Web and notebook scenes capture at build time and require Chrome (via the `nokap` capture library).
If Chrome is not available, the scene renders as a labeled placeholder and the rest of the reel
still builds.
:::

Web scenes take the most effort to author, since you script the interaction, but they are also the
most convincing kind of demo. Author them last, once the surrounding narration is settled, so you
only capture the page once.

### Notebook scenes

A notebook scene runs a computation and shows its output, which is ideal for data work where the
result is the whole point. Showreel executes a marimo notebook at build time and captures what it
renders.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-notebook" >}}

One notebook scene: a marimo notebook executed and captured to frames. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Notebook"
  theme: auto
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: nb
    type: notebook
    notebook: reel-notebook.py
    runtime: marimo
    viewport: { width: 1100, height: 720 }
    capture: { mode: full, settle_ms: 1500 }
    say: "A marimo notebook, executed at build time and captured as the scene."
```
:::

A notebook scene executes a [marimo](https://marimo.io) notebook and captures the rendered result.
Point `notebook` at the `.py` file:

```{.yaml}
- id: analysis
  type: notebook
  notebook: notebooks/analysis.py
  runtime: marimo
  say: "The notebook runs top to bottom, and we capture the output."
```

A notebook scene lets a reel prove that the code actually runs. The output you see was produced by
the notebook during the build, not pasted in by hand, so it can never fall out of step with the code.

## The Spec File

So far you have met the scene types one at a time. A spec file gathers them into a single document,
along with the settings that apply to the whole reel. One file is the entire source of a reel, which
makes it easy to read, to diff in version control, and to hand to an assistant. A complete spec has a
top-level block for reel-wide settings, then the `scenes` list. This example shows the common options
in one place:

```{.yaml filename="reels/tour.showreel.yml"}
showreel:
  title: "Product tour"
  aspect: "16:9"            # 16:9 | 9:16 | 1:1 | 4:3
  theme: auto              # auto | light | dark
  brand:
    accent: "#2563eb"
    logo: assets/logo.svg

voice:
  engine: piper
  name: "en_US-amy-medium"

music:
  file: assets/ambient.mp3
  gain_db: -22
  fade_in: 0.5

sfx:
  enabled: true
  transition: whoosh
  cursor_click: click

defaults:
  transition: crossfade
  captions: true
  motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.06 }

scenes:
  - id: intro
    type: title
    title: "Product tour"
    say: "Let me show you around."
```

Settings under `defaults` apply to every scene unless a scene overrides them. Keeping the reel-wide
choices in `showreel`, `voice`, `music`, and `defaults` means each scene only has to state what makes
it different, which is what keeps a long reel readable.

## Narration and Voice

Narration is what turns a slideshow into a demo. You write each line as plain text, and Showreel
speaks it, so there is no recording booth, no microphone, and no re-takes when the script changes.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-voice" >}}

Two narrated scenes; every line of the voiceover is synthesized locally. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Narration"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { transition: crossfade, captions: true , lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: t
    type: title
    title: "A generated voiceover"
    subtitle: "no recording booth required"
    say: "Every scene's narration is synthesized with a local voice, so the whole reel speaks for itself."
    motion: { type: none }
  - id: c
    type: card
    title: "Just write the lines"
    body: "the build produces the audio"
    say: "You write the lines; the build turns them into speech and times the scenes to match."
```
:::

Each scene's `say` value becomes a line of narration. Showreel synthesizes it with a pluggable
text-to-speech engine and derives the scene's duration from the spoken length, so you write in
sentences rather than timestamps.

The available engines are:

| Engine   | Description                                                        |
| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `piper`  | Local, offline, and free. The default. Requires the `piper` binary |
| `silent` | No audio; estimates timing from word count. The automatic fallback |

Set the engine and voice globally under `voice`, and override per scene when you want a different
speaker:

```{.yaml}
voice:
  engine: piper
  name: "en_US-amy-medium"

scenes:
  - id: q
    type: card
    say: "Any questions?"
    voice: { name: "en_US-ryan-high" }
```

:::{.callout-tip}
You do not need a voice engine installed to build a reel. With no `piper` binary present, Showreel
uses the `silent` engine: the player still shows scenes, timing, and captions, just without audio.
Run `great-docs showreel voices` to see which engines are available.
:::

To narrate reels with a local voice, install Piper and download a voice into a
`.piper-voices/` directory at your project root. The build finds it automatically:

```bash
pip install piper-tts
python -m piper.download_voices en_US-amy-medium --data-dir .piper-voices
```

(Alternatively, set `SHOWREEL_PIPER_DATA_DIR` to any directory of voices.) If no voice
is available at build time, reels fall back to silent audio and still render.

Because the audio is generated from your text, editing a line is as cheap as editing a sentence. You
rewrite the `say` value, rebuild, and the voiceover and the scene timing update together.

### Pacing with a lead-in

A reel that starts talking the instant it appears can feel abrupt. Give each scene a short beat of
dead air before the narration begins with `lead_in` (seconds), the way Keynote lets a build start
after a delay. The visual and its motion appear right away; the voice and captions hold until the
lead-in elapses. Set it per scene, or once in `defaults` to pace the whole reel:

```{.yaml}
defaults:
  lead_in: 0.5             # a half-second beat before each scene speaks

scenes:
  - id: intro
    type: title
    title: "data_color()"
    say: "Great Tables turns a column of numbers into a color gradient."
  - id: hero
    type: image
    src: skyline.png
    lead_in: 1.2           # let the establishing shot breathe before speaking
    say: "Here is the result."
```

Values around `0.4` to `0.6` read as a natural presenter's pause. On an auto-duration scene the
lead-in is added on top so the full line still plays; on a scene with an explicit `duration` it lives
inside that time. The lead-in carries through to exported video, so the web player and the MP4 stay
in sync. Leave it unset (the default is `0`) to keep the immediate start.

A lead-in is a small touch, but it is the difference between a reel that feels rushed and one that
feels composed. Set it once in `defaults` and every scene inherits the same measured opening.

## Captions

Captions make a reel work with the sound off, which is how most people first meet a video, in a quiet
office or a muted browser tab. Showreel builds them from the same narration text, so they are always
in sync and never drift.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-captions" >}}

A single narrated scene, used here to show the word-synced captions. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Karaoke captions"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { transition: crossfade, captions: true , lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: t
    type: title
    title: "Word-synced captions"
    subtitle: "each word lights up as it is spoken"
    say: "Captions are word synced. Watch each word brighten exactly as the narration reaches it."
    motion: { type: none }
```
:::

Captions are on by default and are word-synced. Each word brightens as the narration reaches it, and
the word currently being spoken takes the brand accent color. Because captions are driven by the
clock, they read the same in the web player and the exported video.

Turn captions off for a specific scene with `captions: false`, or hide them in the player with the
CC button.

Leave captions on by default. They cost you nothing to produce, they make the reel accessible to
screen readers and to viewers who cannot use audio, and they keep your message legible in a silent
tab.

## Motion and Transitions

Stillness reads as a slide; movement reads as video. Motion adds a slow drift or push to otherwise
static scenes, and transitions decide how one scene hands off to the next. Together they give a reel
the gentle momentum that keeps a viewer watching.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-motion" >}}

Three image scenes, each carrying a different camera move. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Motion"
  theme: auto
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
# A silent auto-loop: it plays when scrolled into view and pauses when it leaves.
player: { autoplay: true, loop: true }
defaults: { transition: crossfade, captions: true , lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: a
    type: image
    src: city-zurich.jpg
    say: "A pan and zoom drifts across the frame."
    motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.1, from: top-left, to: bottom-right }
  - id: b
    type: image
    src: city-singapore.jpg
    say: "Zoom pushes straight in."
    motion: { type: scale, zoom: 1.16 }
  - id: c
    type: image
    src: city-chicago-panoramic-skyline.png
    say: "And a hard cut lands on the final shot."
    transition: cut
    motion: { type: move, zoom: 1.12, from: left, to: right }
```
:::

Motion keeps static scenes alive. Set a scene's `motion` to one of `pan-zoom`, `scale`, `move`, or
`none`, following Keynote's Action-effect names (`scale` and `move`), with `pan-zoom` for the
combined drift and zoom. The `from` and `to` anchors accept `center`, the four corners, and the four
edges:

```{.yaml}
motion: { type: pan-zoom, zoom: 1.08, from: bottom-left, to: top-right }
```

Transitions control how one scene gives way to the next. Set `transition` to `crossfade` (a smooth
dissolve) or `cut` (an instant change). The crossfade duration is configurable with
`transition_duration`.

Used lightly, motion and crossfades give a reel a calm, produced feel. Used heavily, they distract,
so pick one gentle move per scene and let the content carry the rest.

## Overlays and Cursor

When a scene is busy, overlays and a cursor tell the viewer exactly where to look. You can dim
everything but one region, label a detail, or glide a pointer to the element you are describing, so
the words and the picture point at the same thing.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-overlays" >}}

One image scene annotated with overlays and a synthetic cursor. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Overlays and cursor"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: shot
    type: image
    src: great-docs-site.png
    duration: 7.0
    motion: { type: none }
    say: "Spotlight a region, glide a cursor in, and drop a callout to guide the eye."
    cursor:
      - { at: 0.3, x: 0.22, y: 0.5 }
      - { at: 2.4, x: 0.5, y: 0.42, click: true }
    overlays:
      - { at: 0.6, duration: 5.5, type: spotlight, rect: [0.05, 0.28, 0.24, 0.5] }
      - { at: 2.6, duration: 3.5, type: callout, rect: [0.05, 0.28, 0.24, 0.12], text: "the hero", color: "#8be9fd" }
```
:::

Overlays draw the viewer's eye to part of a scene. Add an `overlays` list, positioning each with a
normalized rectangle (`rect: [x, y, width, height]`, all from 0 to 1) and a time window relative to
the scene start:

```{.yaml}
overlays:
  - { at: 1.0, duration: 3.0, type: spotlight, rect: [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2] }
  - { at: 2.0, duration: 2.5, type: callout, rect: [0.1, 0.3, 0.4, 0.2], text: "the title" }
```

The overlay types are `spotlight` (dims everything else), `box` (an outlined rectangle), `callout`
(a box with a labeled pill), and `label` (a floating pill).

For web scenes, `cursor: synthetic` generates a pointer path automatically from your click steps. On
any scene you can define the path by hand with a list of waypoints:

```{.yaml}
cursor:
  - { at: 0.2, x: 0.15, y: 0.7 }
  - { at: 1.6, x: 0.55, y: 0.5, click: true }
```

The pointer eases between waypoints, and a ripple animates wherever `click: true` is set.

Reach for these when a word in the narration points at something on screen. A spotlight or a moving
cursor closes the gap between what you say and what the viewer sees, without you having to describe a
location in words.

### Region callouts on an output

To explain a rendered result, like a specific column or spanner of a Great Tables output, add an
`annotate` list to the scene. Each entry marks a rectangle of the visual and shows a note that
auto-dodges into the clear space beside it, so the note never covers the data it describes. The rect
is relative to the image, so it lands on the right part of a table regardless of letterboxing, and
the note text takes inline Markdown:

```{.yaml}
- id: result
  type: image
  src: assets/table.png
  fit: contain
  annotate:
    - rect: [0.44, 0.04, 0.26, 0.20]     # the Population spanner
      note: "`tab_spanner()` groups two columns under one header."
      at: 0.6
      duration: 2.6
    - rect: [0.20, 0.04, 0.24, 0.20]     # the Location spanner
      note: "`md(\"*Location*\")` even italicizes the label."
      at: 3.4
```

Give each callout an `at` and `duration` to reveal them in sequence, one part of the output at a
time. Placement is automatic; `side: top | bottom | left | right` overrides it. The opening Code
scene's output table above uses two of these callouts.

## Music and Sound Effects

Sound sets the mood. A quiet music bed under the narration and a soft cue at each transition make a
reel feel finished. You supply one audio file for the bed, and the transition and click effects are
synthesized at build time, so there are no effect files to hunt down or license.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-music" >}}

Two scenes over a looping music bed, with a synthesized transition whoosh. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Music and sound"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb" }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
music: { file: reel-ambient.mp3, gain_db: -20, fade_in: 0.6, fade_out: 1.2 }
sfx: { enabled: true, transition: whoosh, gain_db: -8 }
defaults: { transition: crossfade, captions: true , lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: t
    type: title
    title: "Music and sound"
    subtitle: "an ambient bed, ducked under narration"
    say: "A music bed plays underneath, and automatically ducks while the narration speaks."
    motion: { type: none }
  - id: c
    type: card
    title: "Whoosh"
    body: "synthesized transition sounds"
    say: "And each transition gets a synthesized whoosh."
```
:::

A background music bed plays under the whole reel. Point `music.file` at an audio file and set
`gain_db` to taste. In the exported video, the music is automatically ducked (lowered) while
narration plays:

```{.yaml}
music:
  file: assets/ambient.mp3
  gain_db: -22
  fade_in: 0.5
  fade_out: 1.5
```

Sound effects are synthesized at build time, so there are no audio files to manage. Enable them
under `sfx`, choosing a `transition` sound played at each scene change and a `cursor_click` sound
played at cursor clicks:

```{.yaml}
sfx:
  enabled: true
  transition: whoosh
  cursor_click: click
  gain_db: -8
```

The built-in sounds are `whoosh`, `swoosh`, `click`, `tick`, `pop`, and `ding`. For a one-off cue,
add an `sfx` list to a scene:

```{.yaml}
- id: reveal
  type: card
  title: "Done"
  sfx:
    - { at: 0.4, sound: ding }
```

Keep music low and effects subtle. In the exported video the bed ducks automatically under the
narration, and on the web the effects fire once per cue, so sound supports the voiceover rather than
competing with it.

## Branding

A reel embedded in your docs should look like it belongs there. Branding ties a reel to your project
with a single accent color and a logo, applied everywhere automatically, so a demo never looks like a
generic stock template.

{{< showreel file="assets/reel-branding" >}}

Two scenes carrying a brand logo sting and accent color. Here is the full spec:

::: {.details summary="View the reel spec" icon="code"}
```yaml
showreel:
  title: "Branding"
  theme: auto
  brand: { accent: "#2563eb", logo: logo.svg }
voice: { engine: piper, name: en_US-amy-medium }
defaults: { transition: crossfade, captions: true , lead_in: 0.5 }
scenes:
  - id: t
    type: title
    title: "Great Tables"
    subtitle: "Your logo, your colors"
    say: "Set a brand logo and accent color, and every title card is on brand."
    motion: { type: none }
  - id: c
    type: card
    title: "On brand"
    body: "logo stings and accent color"
    say: "The logo animates in, and the accent colors the captions and controls."
```
:::

Set `brand.accent` to color captions, callouts, and controls with your brand color. Set `brand.logo`
to a logo file, and it animates onto every title and card scene with a subtle fade and scale. The
logo also appears on the social poster.

Set your brand once at the top of the spec, and every title card, caption, control, and poster picks
it up. Nothing else in the reel needs to know your colors.

## The Player

The player is what your readers actually touch, so it is built to be small, dependency-free, and
predictable. It loads fast and behaves the same on every page and in every browser. A reel is a
presentation, not a movie, so the player defaults to **minimal** chrome: a small play button and a
thin progress line, nothing more.

Whatever the chrome, the player renders captions as real text for accessibility, and a playing reel
pauses when it scrolls off-screen even if you started it by hand. Because this one player is also the
renderer behind the video export and the poster, everything you see here is exactly what an exported
file will show. There is no separate video pipeline to drift out of sync.

## Player Settings

You tune the player with a reel-wide `player` block. The defaults suit a lightweight, presentational
reel, so you only set what you want to change:

```{.yaml}
player:
  controls: minimal      # minimal (default) | full | none
  play_button: below     # below (default) | overlay
  loop: false            # restart at the end
  autoplay: false        # play when scrolled into view (muted); pause when it leaves
```

The four settings are:

| Setting        | Values                    | Default   | What it controls                                  |
| -------------- | ------------------------- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `controls`     | `minimal`, `full`, `none` | `minimal` | How much transport chrome the player shows        |
| `play_button`  | `below`, `overlay`        | `below`   | Where the play button sits                        |
| `loop`         | `true`, `false`           | `false`   | Whether the reel restarts at the end              |
| `autoplay`     | `true`, `false`           | `false`   | Whether it plays on scroll-in and pauses off-screen |

**`controls`** sets the chrome level. `minimal` is the slim default (play button plus a thin progress
line). `full` adds the complete transport: a scrubber with a marker at each scene, elapsed time, a
0.5x to 2x speed control, and a captions toggle, along with keyboard control ({{< keys "Space" >}} to
play, {{< keys shortcut="ArrowLeft" >}} / {{< keys shortcut="ArrowRight" >}} to seek, {{< keys "C" >}}
for captions). `none` hides the bar entirely for a chrome-free presentation.

**`play_button`** decides where the play affordance lives: `below` in the control bar, or `overlay`
as a large button on the presentation itself, where clicking anywhere on the frame plays or pauses.

**`loop`** restarts the reel automatically at the end, for a presentation that runs continuously.

**`autoplay`** starts the reel when it scrolls into view and pauses it when it scrolls away, so it is
never running off-screen. Autoplay reels play **muted**, because browsers block autoplaying sound and
a looping presentation reads best silently, so keep a narrated reel click-to-play. Autoplay also
respects a reader's reduced-motion preference, showing a play button instead of starting on its own.

A few common combinations:

```{.yaml}
# The full transport, for a reel meant to be scrubbed and studied.
player: { controls: full }

# A silent, looping presentation that plays as it scrolls into view.
player: { autoplay: true, loop: true }

# A big play button on the frame instead of a control bar.
player: { play_button: overlay, controls: minimal }
```

The reel at the top of this page uses `controls: full`; the image and motion reels above use the
overlay button and the silent auto-loop, respectively.

## Embedding in Pages

Embedding is the whole point: a reel lives inside your documentation, not on a separate video host
that can rate-limit, rebrand, or disappear. One shortcode drops a self-contained player into any
page.

Embed a reel with the shortcode, giving the path to the spec without its extension:

```markdown
{{</* showreel file="reels/getting-started" */>}}
```

During the build, Great Docs discovers every `{{< showreel >}}` shortcode, builds the referenced
reel, and injects a self-contained player. The manifest, images, audio, and player code are all
inlined, so the page makes no runtime network requests and works from a local file.

The `file` option is the path relative to your project root, without the `.showreel.yml` suffix. All
other behavior comes from the reel's own spec.

Because everything is inlined at build time, the page carries no external dependencies. It works
offline, over `file://`, and behind a firewall, with nothing to break or expire after you publish.

## Exporting Video

Sometimes you need a file rather than an embed, for a release announcement, a social post, or a
conference slide. Export produces one from the reel you already have, and because it renders the very
same player, the download matches the page.

The web player is the reference renderer, and the video export captures that same player frame by
frame in headless Chrome, then muxes it with the narration, music, and sound effects using ffmpeg.
The video is therefore identical to the in-page player.

Export to MP4, WebM, or an animated GIF:

```bash
great-docs showreel export reels/tour --format mp4
great-docs showreel export reels/tour --format webm --height 1080
great-docs showreel export reels/tour --format gif
```

Capture a still image for social cards and link previews:

```bash
great-docs showreel poster reels/tour --at 3.5
```

:::{.callout-note}
Video export requires both `ffmpeg` (on your PATH) and Chrome (via `nokap`). The web player and the
`{{< showreel >}}` embed need neither.
:::

Author and preview your reel as an embed, then export only when you need a shareable file. The spec
never changes; only the output format does, so one source can produce a page, a video, and a poster.

## The Studio Editor

Editing YAML by hand is fine for small changes, but a visual editor is faster for arranging a reel
and seeing the result. The Studio editor gives you a live preview while you add, reorder, and tune
scenes. It shows your scenes on the left, a live preview in the center, and a properties panel on the
right. Launch it for any reel:

```bash
great-docs showreel edit reels/tour
```

From the editor you can add, delete, reorder (by dragging), and edit scenes, then save back to the
spec or rebuild the preview. Reordering updates the preview instantly without a full rebuild.

The Studio is also designed to be driven by an AI assistant. It exposes a small command interface
that an agent can call to read the current scenes and apply edits, and it hot-reloads whenever the
spec file changes on disk. A **Copilot** toggle in the toolbar gates agent edits: turn it off during
a hands-on session and the assistant stands down, so you can work without the reel changing under
you. Every agent action is written to the activity log at the bottom of the window, so you always
see what changed.

The Studio is optional. You can author an entire reel in a text editor, but when you want to see
changes as you make them, or hand the reins to an assistant, it is there.

## CLI Reference

Every Showreel task has a command, and they all live under a single group. This table is the quick
reference for the commands used throughout this page:

| Command   | Description                                                    |
| --------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `new`     | Scaffold a starter `NAME.showreel.yml` spec                    |
| `build`   | Build a reel into player assets                                |
| `preview` | Build a reel and serve the player locally                      |
| `export`  | Build and render a video (`--format mp4`, `webm`, or `gif`)    |
| `poster`  | Build and capture a social poster image                        |
| `edit`    | Open the Studio editor                                         |
| `voices`  | List the available voice engines                               |

Run any command with `--help` for its full set of options. Day to day you will lean on `build` and
`preview` while iterating, and reach for `export` and `poster` only when you need files to share.

## How It Works

Showreel follows a "render once, output everywhere" design. The build turns your spec into a
manifest plus a set of captured or generated assets. A single clock-driven web player reads that
manifest and is the one and only renderer:

- The `{{< showreel >}}` shortcode embeds that player, with the manifest and assets inlined.
- The video export drives that same player in headless Chrome, one frame at a time, and hands the
  frames to ffmpeg.
- The poster captures a single frame from that same player.

Because there is one renderer rather than several, the in-page reel, the downloaded video, and the
social poster always match.

## Best Practices

- Keep narration to one or two sentences per scene, and let `duration: auto` pace the scene.
- Add a small `lead_in` (about half a second) so the reel opens on a beat rather than mid-sentence.
- Use real, runnable code in `code` scenes; readers will copy it.
- Reach for overlays and a synthetic cursor to direct attention, but use them sparingly.
- Build offline first (title, card, code, image scenes) and add `web` or `notebook` capture once the
  script is settled.
- Set `brand.accent` and `brand.logo` so reels match the rest of your site.

## Next Steps

To document a terminal session instead of a demo, see
[Terminal Recordings](../user-guide/terminal-recordings.qmd). To embed a plain video file, see
[Videos](../user-guide/videos.qmd). To make an interactive table part of a scene, see
[Table Previews](../user-guide/table-previews.qmd).
